figures.caption: Fig. 1. MF17 during autonomous operation at FS Germany 2025. MF17 is an electric, rear-wheel-drive race car capable of accelerating from 0 to 100 km/h in 3.5 s, with a top speed of 137 km/h. © FS Germany – Photo by Maru
Fig. 8. Friction map overlaid on a drone image of the race track. The track features three asphalt types, making driving particularly challenging.

Fig. 7. Comparison of our approach (green) with a human driver (orange). The top plot shows lap time progression over 10 Trackdrive laps. The bottom plot compares the velocity profiles for the laps with the most similar lap times. The driver’s 2nd lap and our approach’s 8th lap.

Fig. 2. Overview of our Adaptive VP. The system takes the vehicle velocity, position and the curvature of the planned path as inputs, estimates a scaling factor θℓ(d) based on vehicle stability metrics (III-A), updates a continuous friction map (III-B), and computes the acceleration limits based on θ̃ℓ−1(d) (III-C), which are determined for each position d ∈ [d,Λ] along the prediction horizon. These limits are used by a Forward-Backward Solver to compute the velocity profile η(d) (III-D).

Fig. 4. Comparison of our approach (green) with a non-adaptive VP (orange). The top plot compares lap times of the two approaches over 10 Trackdrive laps. The bottom plot compares the velocity profiles of both approaches for the 10th lap.

Fig. 6. Comparison of estimated friction maps for three approaches: Approach 1 estimates a single scaling factor [19], Approach 2 estimates one scaling factor per track segment [20], and Approach 3, our method, continuously estimates the scaling factor at every position.
